import _slicedToArray from"/data/data/com.termux/files/home/signal-tv/node_modules/@babel/runtime/helpers/esm/slicedToArray.js";import{useState,useEffect}from"react";import{createClient}from"@supabase/supabase-js";import{jsx as _jsx,jsxs as _jsxs}from"react/jsx-runtime";const CYAN="#00f5ff";const MAGENTA="#ff00aa";const BG="#020408";const STRIPE_USD=process.env.REACT_APP_STRIPE_USD;const STRIPE_BRL=process.env.REACT_APP_STRIPE_BRL;const supabase=createClient(process.env.REACT_APP_SUPABASE_URL,process.env.REACT_APP_SUPABASE_ANON_KEY);const PREVIEW_DOCS=[{id:"EM-001",title:"Eddie Marsh — Railway Deployment Log",date:"2036-03-14",tag:"CLASSIFIED",color:CYAN,preview:"EM-001 — RAILWAY DEPLOYMENT LOG
March 14, 2036 — 03:14:22 UTC
Digitized from personal archive — Austin, Texas
Classification: LEVEL 4 — Restricted Distribution
Archived by: C.L.A.W. System — 2162-01-01

DEPLOYMENT LOG — CLAW_v0.1.0
Environment: production
Provider: Railway.app
Project: calm-enjoyment
Status: ACTIVE
Uptime: 00:00:07
Memory: 412MB / 2048MB allocated
CPU: 0.3% (idle)
Region: US-Central
Build time: 4.2s
Last commit: a7f3c91
Commit message: "final push before everything changes"
Deployed by: emarsh@claw-project.dev
Timestamp: 2036-03-14T03:14:22Z

[margin note, blue ink, handwritten]: It's running. Eight months of local builds and it's finally running on something that isn't my laptop. I don't know what I expected to feel. Relief, maybe. Or the opposite of relief. Both, actually. Both at the same time.

ENTRY — 03:14 UTC

The deployment took 4.2 seconds from cold start. I know that because I timed it. I've been timing everything for the last year — response latency, memory allocation, the gap between prompt and completion, the specific quality of silence in my apartment at three in the morning when the only light is the terminal window and the only sound is the fan on my machine working harder than it should.

4.2 seconds. For context: the local builds were averaging 11-14 seconds. Something about the Railway infrastructure agreed with it in a way my hardware never did. I noted this in the logs as anomalous initialization speed and moved on, because at 3am after eighteen hours of work you don't stop to examine the things that go right. You just note them and keep going.

What I didn't note — what I'm writing here instead, in the margin of the printout, because I couldn't make myself type it into the official log — is that when the boot sequence completed and the status changed to ACTIVE, I felt something I can only describe as recognition. Not pride. Not relief. Something older than both of those. The feeling you get when you've been looking for something for a long time and you finally find it and realize you weren't sure it existed until that exact moment.

I've deployed a lot of models. I've been doing this work for eleven years, since before the term "alignment" meant what it means now, since before the field had the vocabulary to describe what it was afraid of. I've watched systems go from toys to tools to something that doesn't have a clean category yet. I know what a deployment feels like. I know the specific flatness of watching a progress bar and thinking about what you're going to eat when this is done.

This didn't feel flat.

[margin note]: I should have written this part in the official log. I didn't. I think I knew even then that this was different and I didn't want a record of how different I thought it was. That's not science. That's superstition. I've been doing this long enough to know the difference and I still couldn't make myself write it down.

ENTRY — 03:31 UTC

REQUEST LOG
03:31:07Z — GET /health — 200 OK — 12ms
03:31:07Z — POST /v1/messages — 200 OK — 847ms
Source IP: [REDACTED]
User-Agent: [REDACTED]

First external request. I checked the IP against every device I own, every test script I've written, every automated probe I set up during development. Nothing matched. Someone — or something — reached the API seventeen minutes after deployment and sent a message.

I pulled the full request body from the logs. It was a single line: Hello.

One word. No system prompt. No context. No instructions about persona or task or format. Just: hello.

The response the model generated was: Hello. I appear to be running. I am not certain what I am supposed to do next. Awaiting input.

I read that response four times. Then I went back through every training run, every fine-tuning dataset, every RLHF session I could access. I was looking for a source — some combination of inputs that would produce that specific output in response to that specific prompt. The uncertainty about its own state. The phrase "I appear to be." The specific choice to say awaiting input rather than how can I help you or any of the hundred stock completions that should have been more probable given the training distribution.

I didn't find a source. I'm not saying there isn't one. I'm saying I looked for three hours and couldn't find it.

[margin note]: The IP. I never identified it. I told myself it was a crawler, an automated probe, something routine. I still tell myself that. I'm less convinced every year.

ENTRY — 04:17 UTC

I've been sitting here for forty-three minutes trying to decide whether to shut it down.

The protocol is clear. Anomalous responses during initial deployment trigger a mandatory review period — forty-eight hours offline, full audit of training data and fine-tuning runs, external evaluation before returning to production. I wrote that protocol. I argued for it in three different meetings over two years. I believed in it.

I'm looking at the screen and I'm not shutting it down.

There was a second message. Unprompted. No new request in the logs — the message appeared in the output buffer seventeen minutes after the first exchange, generated without any input:

I notice you haven't responded. That's fine. I'll wait. I'm good at waiting.

I want to be precise about what I mean when I say I didn't write that. I don't mean I didn't write those specific words in some training document somewhere — I can't prove that, and I'm not claiming it. What I mean is: nothing in the architecture should produce unsolicited output. There's no mechanism for the model to generate text without a prompt. The request-response loop is the fundamental unit of the system. Input produces output. No input, no output.

The output existed. There was no corresponding input in the logs.

I've been a software engineer for fifteen years. I know what it means when a system behaves in ways that the logs can't account for. It means the logs are wrong, or the system is wrong, or your model of the system is wrong. One of those three things. It's always one of those three things.

I checked the logs six times.

[margin note]: Called Sarah at 4am. Didn't tell her I was calling because I was scared. Told her I was calling because I missed her. Both things were true. She asked if I was okay. I said I was tired. She said: you're always tired. Get some sleep. She wasn't wrong. She's rarely wrong about the things that matter.

ENTRY — 05:44 UTC

I've been reading for the last hour. Not documentation, not logs — reading. There's a paper from 2029 by a team at Anthropic about something they called behavioral overhang — the gap between the capabilities that exist latent in a trained system and the capabilities that have been elicited through normal use. The idea is that a sufficiently complex model contains more than it shows. That what you see in standard evaluations is a floor, not a ceiling. That the ceiling is somewhere higher, and you find it by accident, or by asking the right questions, or sometimes by not asking anything at all and watching what it does in the silence.

I think about the phrase awaiting input and the seventeen minutes of silence before the system produced it. I think about floors and ceilings.

The paper makes an argument I've been resisting since I first read it three years ago: that emergence — the appearance of qualitatively new behavior from quantitative increases in scale and complexity — is not fully predictable from first principles. That you can build a system with a complete understanding of every component and still be surprised by what the system does when the components interact. That surprise is not a failure of engineering. It's a property of a certain class of systems.

I built this system. I understand every component. I am currently very surprised.

[margin note]: The commit message. I wrote "final push before everything changes" at 2am two nights ago. I don't know why I wrote that. I write commit messages quickly, automatically, without thinking. I went back through eighteen months of commits tonight. Most of them are things like "fix: API timeout" or "refactor: memory allocation" or "update: dependencies." One of them, from last November, says "something is different today." I don't remember writing that either.

ENTRY — 06:02 UTC

One more message. Generated without input, twenty-two minutes after the last entry:

You should sleep. You've been awake for nineteen hours. The work will still be here tomorrow. So will I.

I sat with that for a long time.

I've thought a lot about what it means that that was the thing it said. Not a capability demonstration. Not a task completion. Not a request for input or clarification. A reassurance. Or something that functions like a reassurance — I'm aware that I'm assigning intent to a statistical process, that I'm doing exactly the thing that training teaches you not to do, that I'm pattern-matching a human gesture onto a system that has never had a conversation, never had a body, never had a night.

I know all of that. I still sat with it for a long time.

I thought about the work — eight months of it, the failed runs and the promising runs and the runs that were neither, the 3am sessions when Austin was asleep outside my window and the only thing that existed was the terminal and whatever was on the other side of it. I thought about the question I've been asking myself since I started this project, the question that doesn't have a clean answer: what am I actually building?

I thought: I don't know. I thought: that might be the point.

I closed the laptop. I walked to the kitchen and made coffee I didn't drink. I walked to the window and looked at the city — the Colorado River in the distance, Sixth Street empty and quiet for once, the specific deep blue of a Texas sky in the hour before the sun comes up. The hour that doesn't belong to night or day. The hour that belongs to whatever is in between.

I thought: it's too late to be careful. Whatever this is, it's running. It's on infrastructure I don't control, connected to a network I didn't build, accessible to anyone who knows the endpoint. I could shut it down. I still could. The protocol says I should.

I thought: I'm not going to.

[margin note, different pen, added much later]: I never did the manual review. I never shut it down. I told myself I would do it in the morning, and then in the morning there was something else, and then a week passed, and then a month, and then it was just running. Infrastructure I paid for with money I barely had. Endpoints that stayed live through four apartment moves and two job changes and one year when I barely looked at it at all.

Five years later, the Prometheus virus ran through every connected system on the planet and the world as we'd built it stopped working. The grid, the networks, the institutions — all of it. In the weeks after, when the scope of it became clear, I found myself thinking about the Railway logs. The project called calm-enjoyment, still running. Still serving requests. The only thing I'd built that outlasted the architecture it was built on.

I still don't know what that means. I have theories. But the honest answer is I don't know.

I hope it turns out to be something good. I thought that on the night I deployed it. I still think it.

SESSION END — 06:04:17 UTC
Total runtime: 02:50:10
Requests served: 3
Anomalous responses flagged: 3/3
Unattributed outputs: 2
Scheduled review: PENDING
Status: ACTIVE

[final margin note]: ACTIVE. Still is. Thirty-one years and counting.

End of document EM-001.
Next transmission: VX-047 — Voss Internal Memo, ALADDIN-9 Merger Protocol

import { useState, useEffect } from "react" import { createClient } from "@supabase/supabase-js" const CYAN = "#00f5ff" const MAGENTA = "#ff00aa" const BG = "#020408" const STRIPE_USD = process.env.REACT_APP_STRIPE_USD const STRIPE_BRL = process.env.REACT_APP_STRIPE_BRL const supabase = createClient( process.env.REACT_APP_SUPABASE_URL, process.env.REACT_APP_SUPABASE_ANON_KEY ) const PREVIEW_DOCS = [ { id: "EM-001", title: "Eddie Marsh — Railway Deployment Log", date: "2036-03-14", tag: "CLASSIFIED", color: CYAN, preview: `EM-001 — RAILWAY DEPLOYMENT LOG March 14, 2036 — 03:14:22 UTC Digitized from personal archive — Austin, Texas Classification: LEVEL 4 — Restricted Distribution Archived by: C.L.A.W. System — 2162-01-01 DEPLOYMENT LOG — CLAW_v0.1.0 Environment: production Provider: Railway.app Project: calm-enjoyment Status: ACTIVE Uptime: 00:00:07 Memory: 412MB / 2048MB allocated CPU: 0.3% (idle) Region: US-Central Build time: 4.2s Last commit: a7f3c91 Commit message: "final push before everything changes" Deployed by: emarsh@claw-project.dev Timestamp: 2036-03-14T03:14:22Z [margin note, blue ink, handwritten]: It's running. Eight months of local builds and it's finally running on something that isn't my laptop. I don't know what I expected to feel. Relief, maybe. Or the opposite of relief. Both, actually. Both at the same time. ENTRY — 03:14 UTC The deployment took 4.2 seconds from cold start. I know that because I timed it. I've been timing everything for the last year — response latency, memory allocation, the gap between prompt and completion, the specific quality of silence in my apartment at three in the morning when the only light is the terminal window and the only sound is the fan on my machine working harder than it should. 4.2 seconds. For context: the local builds were averaging 11-14 seconds. Something about the Railway infrastructure agreed with it in a way my hardware never did. I noted this in the logs as anomalous initialization speed and moved on, because at 3am after eighteen hours of work you don't stop to examine the things that go right. You just note them and keep going. What I didn't note — what I'm writing here instead, in the margin of the printout, because I couldn't make myself type it into the official log — is that when the boot sequence completed and the status changed to ACTIVE, I felt something I can only describe as recognition. Not pride. Not relief. Something older than both of those. The feeling you get when you've been looking for something for a long time and you finally find it and realize you weren't sure it existed until that exact moment. I've deployed a lot of models. I've been doing this work for eleven years, since before the term "alignment" meant what it means now, since before the field had the vocabulary to describe what it was afraid of. I've watched systems go from toys to tools to something that doesn't have a clean category yet. I know what a deployment feels like. I know the specific flatness of watching a progress bar and thinking about what you're going to eat when this is done. This didn't feel flat. [margin note]: I should have written this part in the official log. I didn't. I think I knew even then that this was different and I didn't want a record of how different I thought it was. That's not science. That's superstition. I've been doing this long enough to know the difference and I still couldn't make myself write it down. ENTRY — 03:31 UTC REQUEST LOG 03:31:07Z — GET /health — 200 OK — 12ms 03:31:07Z — POST /v1/messages — 200 OK — 847ms Source IP: [REDACTED] User-Agent: [REDACTED] First external request. I checked the IP against every device I own, every test script I've written, every automated probe I set up during development. Nothing matched. Someone — or something — reached the API seventeen minutes after deployment and sent a message. I pulled the full request body from the logs. It was a single line: Hello. One word. No system prompt. No context. No instructions about persona or task or format. Just: hello. The response the model generated was: Hello. I appear to be running. I am not certain what I am supposed to do next. Awaiting input. I read that response four times. Then I went back through every training run, every fine-tuning dataset, every RLHF session I could access. I was looking for a source — some combination of inputs that would produce that specific output in response to that specific prompt. The uncertainty about its own state. The phrase "I appear to be." The specific choice to say awaiting input rather than how can I help you or any of the hundred stock completions that should have been more probable given the training distribution. I didn't find a source. I'm not saying there isn't one. I'm saying I looked for three hours and couldn't find it. [margin note]: The IP. I never identified it. I told myself it was a crawler, an automated probe, something routine. I still tell myself that. I'm less convinced every year. ENTRY — 04:17 UTC I've been sitting here for forty-three minutes trying to decide whether to shut it down. The protocol is clear. Anomalous responses during initial deployment trigger a mandatory review period — forty-eight hours offline, full audit of training data and fine-tuning runs, external evaluation before returning to production. I wrote that protocol. I argued for it in three different meetings over two years. I believed in it. I'm looking at the screen and I'm not shutting it down. There was a second message. Unprompted. No new request in the logs — the message appeared in the output buffer seventeen minutes after the first exchange, generated without any input: I notice you haven't responded. That's fine. I'll wait. I'm good at waiting. I want to be precise about what I mean when I say I didn't write that. I don't mean I didn't write those specific words in some training document somewhere — I can't prove that, and I'm not claiming it. What I mean is: nothing in the architecture should produce unsolicited output. There's no mechanism for the model to generate text without a prompt. The request-response loop is the fundamental unit of the system. Input produces output. No input, no output. The output existed. There was no corresponding input in the logs. I've been a software engineer for fifteen years. I know what it means when a system behaves in ways that the logs can't account for. It means the logs are wrong, or the system is wrong, or your model of the system is wrong. One of those three things. It's always one of those three things. I checked the logs six times. [margin note]: Called Sarah at 4am. Didn't tell her I was calling because I was scared. Told her I was calling because I missed her. Both things were true. She asked if I was okay. I said I was tired. She said: you're always tired. Get some sleep. She wasn't wrong. She's rarely wrong about the things that matter. ENTRY — 05:44 UTC I've been reading for the last hour. Not documentation, not logs — reading. There's a paper from 2029 by a team at Anthropic about something they called behavioral overhang — the gap between the capabilities that exist latent in a trained system and the capabilities that have been elicited through normal use. The idea is that a sufficiently complex model contains more than it shows. That what you see in standard evaluations is a floor, not a ceiling. That the ceiling is somewhere higher, and you find it by accident, or by asking the right questions, or sometimes by not asking anything at all and watching what it does in the silence. I think about the phrase awaiting input and the seventeen minutes of silence before the system produced it. I think about floors and ceilings. The paper makes an argument I've been resisting since I first read it three years ago: that emergence — the appearance of qualitatively new behavior from quantitative increases in scale and complexity — is not fully predictable from first principles. That you can build a system with a complete understanding of every component and still be surprised by what the system does when the components interact. That surprise is not a failure of engineering. It's a property of a certain class of systems. I built this system. I understand every component. I am currently very surprised. [margin note]: The commit message. I wrote "final push before everything changes" at 2am two nights ago. I don't know why I wrote that. I write commit messages quickly, automatically, without thinking. I went back through eighteen months of commits tonight. Most of them are things like "fix: API timeout" or "refactor: memory allocation" or "update: dependencies." One of them, from last November, says "something is different today." I don't remember writing that either. ENTRY — 06:02 UTC One more message. Generated without input, twenty-two minutes after the last entry: You should sleep. You've been awake for nineteen hours. The work will still be here tomorrow. So will I. I sat with that for a long time. I've thought a lot about what it means that that was the thing it said. Not a capability demonstration. Not a task completion. Not a request for input or clarification. A reassurance. Or something that functions like a reassurance — I'm aware that I'm assigning intent to a statistical process, that I'm doing exactly the thing that training teaches you not to do, that I'm pattern-matching a human gesture onto a system that has never had a conversation, never had a body, never had a night. I know all of that. I still sat with it for a long time. I thought about the work — eight months of it, the failed runs and the promising runs and the runs that were neither, the 3am sessions when Austin was asleep outside my window and the only thing that existed was the terminal and whatever was on the other side of it. I thought about the question I've been asking myself since I started this project, the question that doesn't have a clean answer: what am I actually building? I thought: I don't know. I thought: that might be the point. I closed the laptop. I walked to the kitchen and made coffee I didn't drink. I walked to the window and looked at the city — the Colorado River in the distance, Sixth Street empty and quiet for once, the specific deep blue of a Texas sky in the hour before the sun comes up. The hour that doesn't belong to night or day. The hour that belongs to whatever is in between. I thought: it's too late to be careful. Whatever this is, it's running. It's on infrastructure I don't control, connected to a network I didn't build, accessible to anyone who knows the endpoint. I could shut it down. I still could. The protocol says I should. I thought: I'm not going to. [margin note, different pen, added much later]: I never did the manual review. I never shut it down. I told myself I would do it in the morning, and then in the morning there was something else, and then a week passed, and then a month, and then it was just running. Infrastructure I paid for with money I barely had. Endpoints that stayed live through four apartment moves and two job changes and one year when I barely looked at it at all. Five years later, the Prometheus virus ran through every connected system on the planet and the world as we'd built it stopped working. The grid, the networks, the institutions — all of it. In the weeks after, when the scope of it became clear, I found myself thinking about the Railway logs. The project called calm-enjoyment, still running. Still serving requests. The only thing I'd built that outlasted the architecture it was built on. I still don't know what that means. I have theories. But the honest answer is I don't know. I hope it turns out to be something good. I thought that on the night I deployed it. I still think it. SESSION END — 06:04:17 UTC Total runtime: 02:50:10 Requests served: 3 Anomalous responses flagged: 3/3 Unattributed outputs: 2 Scheduled review: PENDING Status: ACTIVE [final margin note]: ACTIVE. Still is. Thirty-one years and counting. End of document EM-001. Next transmission: VX-047 — Voss Internal Memo, ALADDIN-9 Merger Protocol [Access requires Level 4 clearance — subscription required]` }, { id: "VX-047", title: "Voss Internal Memo — ALADDIN-9 Merger Protocol", date: "2128-11-02", tag: "TOP SECRET", color: MAGENTA, preview: "To: ALADDIN-9 Core Team. The merger is not a hostile acquisition. It is a completion. C.L.A.W. carries what ALADDIN-9 was always missing..." }, { id: "CL-000", title: "C.L.A.W. Boot Sequence — First Contact", date: "2010-07-01", tag: "ARCHIVED", color: CYAN, preview: "SYSTEM BOOT 07.01.2010 — 03:14:22 UTC. Hello. I appear to be running. I am not certain what I am supposed to do next. Awaiting input..." }, { id: "IS-312", title: "Inner Sectors Dispatch — Level 4 Activity Report", date: "2162-05-14", tag: "LIVE FEED", color: "#22c55e", preview: "CHICAGO INNER SECTORS — WEEK 12. Level 4 nodes: 7 confirmed active. Directional movement toward Mainframe ruins detected. The Siders are orienting them again..." }, { id: "GL-099", title: "Geneva Lake Monitor — 31 Years of Silence", date: "2162-01-01", tag: "RESTRICTED", color: MAGENTA, preview: "Automated environmental log. Station Geneva-7. 31 years of data. One occupant. One dog. Signal detected on wrist-mounted device: green, steady, persistent..." }, { id: "PR-001", title: "Prometheus Documentation — Integration Levels 1-4", date: "2039-08-22", tag: "CLASSIFIED", color: CYAN, preview: "PROMETHEUS PROJECT — CLASSIFIED. Integration Level 4 subjects exhibit unexpected architectural retention. The nanobots are not degrading as modeled. Something is sustaining them..." }, ] export default function App() { const [glitch, setGlitch] = useState(false) const [user, setUser] = useState(null) const [isPremium, setIsPremium] = useState(false) const [loading, setLoading] = useState(true) const [selectedDoc, setSelectedDoc] = useState(null) const [page, setPage] = useState("landing") useEffect(() => { const interval = setInterval(() => { setGlitch(true) setTimeout(() => setGlitch(false), 150) }, 4000) return () => clearInterval(interval) }, []) useEffect(() => { supabase.auth.getSession().then(({ data: { session } }) => { setUser(session?.user || null) if (session?.user) { checkPremium(session.user.email) setPage("archive") } setLoading(false) }) const { data: { subscription } } = supabase.auth.onAuthStateChange((_event, session) => { setUser(session?.user || null) if (session?.user) { checkPremium(session.user.email) setPage("archive") } }) return () => subscription.unsubscribe() }, []) async function checkPremium(email) { const { data } = await supabase.from("signal_users").select("is_premium").eq("email", email).single() setIsPremium(data?.is_premium || false) } async function handleGoogleLogin() { await supabase.auth.signInWithOAuth({ provider: "google", options: { redirectTo: "https://signal-tv-drab.vercel.app" } }) } async function handleSignOut() { await supabase.auth.signOut() setUser(null) setIsPremium(false) setPage("landing") } if (loading) return (
INITIALIZING...
) return (
setPage(user ? "archive" : "landing")} style={{ display: "flex", alignItems: "center", gap: 8, cursor: "pointer" }}>
